name: Green Tea Extract
historicalUse: See EGCG (Green Tea) monograph for full historical context. The 50% EGCG standardized extract provides a broad-spectrum green tea polyphenol profile while maintaining significant EGCG concentration. This intermediate standardization preserves the synergistic catechin complex (EGCG, EGC, ECG, EC) alongside EGCG, potentially offering broader activity than isolated 98% EGCG.
modernEvidence: 50% EGCG green tea extract provides the full catechin complex alongside EGCG, offering potentially synergistic antioxidant, metabolic, and cellular protective effects. The combination of catechins demonstrates additive and synergistic activity in clinical research on metabolic syndrome, cardiovascular risk markers, and cellular oxidative stress. This standardization is widely used in clinical trials due to its comprehensive polyphenol profile.
mechanisms: Broad-spectrum catechin antioxidant activity — all four major catechins provide complementary radical scavenging
AMPK activation — improves metabolic sensing across tissues
Nrf2 pathway activation — upregulates cellular antioxidant enzyme systems
NF-kB suppression — reduces inflammatory cytokine transcription
Gut microbiome modulation — catechins selectively support beneficial bacteria
Lipid oxidation inhibition — reduces LDL and membrane lipid peroxidation
safetyProfile: Well tolerated at standard doses. Same safety considerations as EGCG monograph — take with food, monitor liver function at high doses long-term. 50% standardization has lower concentrated-EGCG hepatotoxicity risk than 98% isolated EGCG.
contraindications: Iron deficiency anemia — reduces non-heme iron absorption; separate from iron
Liver disease — monitor LFTs with long-term use at high doses
drugInteractions: CYP3A4 substrates — mild inhibition at high doses
physicianNotes: The 50% EGCG extract is appropriate in standard cellular support protocols. Always taken with food. Lower hepatotoxicity risk than 98% isolated EGCG due to lower concentrated-EGCG per dose. Iron absorption interference is the most practically relevant interaction in clinical practice.
patientGuidance: Take with meals. If you take iron supplements, separate the doses by at least 2 hours. If you have liver disease or take blood thinners, inform your physician.
